name: IP Addressing
description: Quiz focused on the IPv4 and IPv6 protocols, logical address resolution, spoofing and ports.

questions.question: In computer network infrastructure, what is the best definition of an IP address?
options: It is a logical address that uniquely identifies a device within the network.
It is a unique physical address assigned at the factory to each access device.
It is an encrypted digital signature that securely validates its identity.
It is a communication port dedicated exclusively to application traffic.

questions.question: What is the structural and visual standard universally adopted to represent the IPv4 protocol?
options: It is made of four blocks of numbers, from zero to 255, separated by dots.
It is made of eight groups of hexadecimal characters separated by commas.
It is made of sixteen continuous alphanumeric blocks separated by single dashes.
It is made of 64-bit binary numbers split into three equally sized blocks.

questions.question: IPv6 emerged to expand the number of addresses available on the network. What is its main format?
options: It has eight groups of hexadecimal characters strictly separated by dots.
It has eight groups of hexadecimal characters separated by colons.
It has four 32-bit numeric groups separated by straight dashes.
It has twelve groupings of alphanumeric text separated by slashes.

questions.question: In a device's network configuration, what is the role played by the 'Default Gateway' address?
options: It is the IP address of the local switch, restricted to delivering internal network frames.
It is the IP address of the web server that hosts your organization's main page.
It is the IP address of the router used to forward data between different networks.
It is the IP address of the DNS in charge of translating names into domains.

questions.question: There is a fundamental difference between the 'Unicast' and 'Multicast' modes. What characterizes a Multicast address?
options: It identifies exclusively and directly a single device or interface.
It identifies an entire group of connected devices simultaneously.
It acts as a barrier that blocks unwanted external access.
It masks the sender's original IP to provide confidentiality of use.

questions.question: How is the destination IP address contained in the header used by routers?
options: It is read by the router in its table to map the best link for the exit.
It is changed by the router at each hop to prevent attacks from malicious networks.
It is fragmented by the router so it can be sent over several ports in parallel.
It is discarded at the edge right after the packet manages to reach its output link.

questions.question: What perfectly characterizes an IP Spoofing attack?
options: The injection of packets that carry a maliciously forged source IP address.
The silent reading of all IPs contained in the headers traveling over the link.
The intentional congestion of links by flooding the target IP with fake connections.
The interception of packets in transit to modify their transport ports.

questions.question: At the Transport layer (using Sockets), why is having only the destination IP address not enough to deliver a message?
options: Because the IP points to the router, and the MAC is needed to reach the card.
Because the IP defines only the machine, and the port number is required for the socket.
Because the IP address is dynamic, requiring a constant TLS certificate for the route.
Because the IP changes a lot in switches, requiring a check at the end of the network.

questions.question: In the Internet architecture, what is the exact functional and dependency relationship between the DNS application protocol and IP?
options: DNS adds an extensive extra layer of encryption on top of the IP datagram.
DNS fragments IP packets to ensure that they all fit into the primary link.
DNS maps textual domain names to their IP addresses.
DNS replaces the use of conventional IP addresses to speed up routes.

questions.question: Analyzing the historical limitations and the structure of the global routing architecture, it is correct to state that the IPv4 network protocol:
options: Was built using a native architecture with 32-bit addressing fields.
Was standardized globally to allocate long and unique hexadecimal addresses.
Was abandoned at the end of the 1990s due to the complete fragility of routers.
Was designed to allow the handshake to be established in just a single direct path.
